presentations.arrival: 55M, acute onset substernal pressure, diaphoretic, clutching chest

diagnostics.lookingFor: ST elevation in 2+ contiguous leads with reciprocal changes
Rising pattern confirms myocardial necrosis; may be normal early

differentials.distinguishing: ST elevation + reciprocal changes on ECG; acute onset with diaphoresis; rising troponin

treatments.intervention: Activate cath lab — PCI within 90 min of first medical contact
treatments.why: Mechanical reperfusion physically opens the blocked artery; time is muscle
Irreversible COX-1 inhibition prevents platelet aggregation at the clot; chewing speeds absorption
Prevents clot extension and new thrombus formation while awaiting cath lab
Dual antiplatelet therapy — blocks the second major platelet activation pathway (ADP receptor)

treatments.med: Nitroglycerin SL (if not inferior MI, not hypotensive)
treatments.why: Coronary vasodilation + venodilation decreases preload and myocardial oxygen demand

diagnostics.lookingFor: Filling defect in pulmonary artery confirming clot
Elevated fibrin degradation products; sensitive but not specific — useful to rule OUT PE
Sinus tach most common; S1Q3T3 pattern (classic but uncommon); right heart strain

differentials.distinguishing: Pleuritic chest pain (worse with breathing); acute dyspnea out of proportion to exam; risk factors (immobility, surgery, OCP, cancer); tachycardia with clear lungs

treatments.why: Prevents clot propagation; does not dissolve existing clot but lets body's fibrinolysis work

treatments.intervention: tPA (alteplase) for massive PE with hemodynamic instability
treatments.why: Fibrinolytic directly dissolves clot when patient is in shock from PE

treatments.intervention: Supplemental O2 and hemodynamic support
treatments.why: V/Q mismatch causes hypoxemia; may need fluids cautiously (RV is preload-sensitive but can fail with overload)

diagnostics.lookingFor: Intimal flap separating true and false lumen
Widened mediastinum (>8cm); not always present
>20mmHg difference suggests dissection extending to subclavian artery

differentials.distinguishing: Sudden tearing/ripping pain radiating to back; maximal at onset (not crescendo); BP differential between arms; pulse deficits; hypertension

treatments.why: Rate AND BP control — must reduce aortic wall shear stress; target HR <60, SBP 100-120

treatments.intervention: Emergent surgical consult for Type A (ascending)
treatments.why: Type A dissections have high mortality without surgery; Type B may be managed medically

treatments.intervention: Pain control (morphine/fentanyl)
treatments.why: Pain drives sympathetic response which worsens dissection; controlling pain helps control BP

diagnostics.test: Clinical diagnosis (do NOT delay for imaging)
diagnostics.lookingFor: Absent breath sounds unilaterally, tracheal deviation, JVD, hypotension, tachycardia
Absent lung sliding on affected side; barcode sign on M-mode
Absent lung markings, mediastinal shift away from affected side

differentials.distinguishing: Acute dyspnea + unilateral absent breath sounds + hemodynamic instability; often post-trauma or post-procedure; tracheal deviation is a late sign

treatments.intervention: Needle decompression (14g needle, 2nd ICS midclavicular or 5th ICS anterior axillary)
treatments.why: Immediately releases trapped air under pressure that is compressing the heart and great vessels

treatments.intervention: Chest tube placement (follows needle decompression)
treatments.why: Definitive management — continuous drainage prevents reaccumulation

presentations.arrival: 72F, progressive dyspnea x 3 days, worse lying flat, can't walk to bathroom without stopping

diagnostics.lookingFor: BNP >400 strongly suggests HF; helps differentiate cardiac vs pulmonary dyspnea
Cardiomegaly, pulmonary edema (cephalization, Kerley B lines, pleural effusions)
Afib, LVH, ischemic changes — identify the trigger

differentials.distinguishing: Orthopnea, PND, bilateral crackles, JVD, peripheral edema, S3 gallop. History of HF, missed meds, dietary indiscretion (salt). BNP markedly elevated.

treatments.why: Loop diuretic removes excess volume — works in 5 min IV. Goal: net negative fluid balance
Venodilation decreases preload rapidly — redistributes fluid out of lungs. First-line for acute pulmonary edema

treatments.intervention: BiPAP (non-invasive ventilation)
treatments.why: Positive pressure splints open alveoli, reduces work of breathing, decreases preload and afterload. Buys time while diuretics work

diagnostics.lookingFor: Hypercapnia (elevated PaCO2), respiratory acidosis — indicates ventilatory failure
Hyperinflation, flattened diaphragms. Rule out pneumonia, PTX as triggers

differentials.distinguishing: Known COPD, barrel chest, prolonged expiratory phase, diffuse wheezing or diminished breath sounds, pursed-lip breathing. Triggered by infection, missed inhalers, cold air.

treatments.why: Beta-2 agonist + anticholinergic = dual bronchodilation. Give together for first 3 treatments in severe exacerbation
Systemic corticosteroids reduce airway inflammation — takes 4-6 hours for full effect. 5-day course, no taper needed

treatments.intervention: BiPAP if hypercapnic/tiring
treatments.why: Reduces work of breathing, supports ventilation, avoids intubation. Target SpO2 88-92% — don't over-oxygenate COPD patients

diagnostics.lookingFor: Lobar consolidation, infiltrate, air bronchograms
Elevated suggests bacterial infection; helps guide antibiotic decisions
Identify organism and sensitivities for targeted therapy

differentials.distinguishing: Productive cough, fever, focal crackles on auscultation, pleuritic chest pain. CXR shows infiltrate. May have leukocytosis.

treatments.why: Standard CAP coverage: ceftriaxone for typical organisms (Strep pneumo, H. flu), azithromycin for atypicals (Mycoplasma, Legionella)

treatments.intervention: Oxygen to maintain SpO2 ≥94%
treatments.why: Pneumonia causes V/Q mismatch → hypoxemia. Supplement oxygen as needed.

diagnostics.lookingFor: Filling defect in pulmonary artery
If low clinical suspicion, negative D-dimer rules out PE

differentials.distinguishing: Acute onset dyspnea + pleuritic chest pain + tachycardia with CLEAR lungs. Risk factors: immobility, recent surgery, OCP, cancer, prior DVT. Hypoxemia out of proportion to exam.

treatments.why: Prevents clot propagation. Start if high clinical suspicion — don't wait for CT

treatments.intervention: tPA for massive PE with hemodynamic instability
treatments.why: Fibrinolytic dissolves clot directly when patient is in obstructive shock from PE

presentations.arrival: 38F, acute onset RLQ pain x 12 hours, nausea, one episode of vomiting, low-grade fever

diagnostics.lookingFor: Enlarged appendix >6mm, periappendiceal fat stranding, appendicolith
Leukocytosis with left shift (elevated WBC, bandemia)
Rule out UTI/kidney stone as mimic. Mild pyuria can occur with appendicitis (inflamed appendix near ureter)

differentials.distinguishing: Classic migration: periumbilical pain → RLQ (McBurney's point). Anorexia, low-grade fever, rebound tenderness, guarding. Pain worsens with movement/coughing.

treatments.intervention: Surgical consult for appendectomy
treatments.why: Definitive treatment is surgical removal — laparoscopic preferred. Do not delay for imaging if clinical picture is clear
Empiric coverage for GI flora. Given pre-op and if perforation suspected

treatments.intervention: NPO, IV fluids, pain management
treatments.why: Prep for OR. Don't withhold pain meds — treated pain doesn't mask surgical exam (evidence-based)

diagnostics.lookingFor: Positive pregnancy test — ALWAYS check in reproductive-age females with abdominal pain
Empty uterus + adnexal mass or free fluid with positive hCG = ectopic until proven otherwise
Baseline H/H for bleeding risk. T&S in case of rupture needing transfusion/surgery

differentials.distinguishing: Reproductive-age female, missed period (may not realize she's pregnant), unilateral pelvic/lower abdominal pain, vaginal bleeding. If RUPTURED: peritoneal signs, tachycardia, hypotension — surgical emergency.

treatments.intervention: Emergent OB/GYN consult if ruptured or hemodynamically unstable
treatments.why: Ruptured ectopic = surgical emergency. Hemoperitoneum can be rapidly fatal
Medical management — inhibits rapidly dividing trophoblastic cells. Avoids surgery in stable, early ectopic

treatments.intervention: 2 large-bore IVs, Type & Crossmatch, RhoGAM if Rh-negative
treatments.why: Prepare for hemorrhage. RhoGAM prevents Rh sensitization in Rh-negative mothers

diagnostics.lookingFor: Elevated ≥3x upper limit of normal = diagnostic. More specific than amylase

diagnostics.test: CT abdomen (with contrast if no AKI)
diagnostics.lookingFor: Pancreatic inflammation, necrosis, peripancreatic fluid collections. Not needed for diagnosis if lipase + clinical picture are clear

differentials.distinguishing: Epigastric pain radiating to back, worse after eating, better leaning forward. Often alcohol or gallstone-related. Severe nausea/vomiting. Lipase markedly elevated.

treatments.intervention: Aggressive IV fluid resuscitation (LR preferred)
treatments.why: Pancreatitis causes massive third-spacing. Early aggressive hydration (250-500 mL/hr initially) improves outcomes

treatments.intervention: Pain control (IV opioids — fentanyl or hydromorphone)
treatments.why: Pancreatitis pain is severe. Morphine is acceptable despite old teaching about sphincter of Oddi spasm (debunked)

treatments.intervention: NPO initially, early enteral feeding when tolerated
treatments.why: Old practice was prolonged NPO. Current evidence supports early oral feeding (within 24-48 hrs) if tolerated — improves gut barrier, reduces infection

presentations.arrival: 78M, found confused and agitated by family, normally alert and oriented, no witnessed event

diagnostics.lookingFor: Glucose <70 mg/dL. ALWAYS check glucose first in AMS — takes 5 seconds and is immediately treatable
Confirm glucose, check electrolytes, renal function

differentials.distinguishing: Diabetic on insulin or sulfonylureas, tremor, diaphoresis, tachycardia, confusion, seizure. May mimic stroke perfectly. FASTEST reversible cause of AMS.

treatments.med: D50 (dextrose 50%) 25g IV push if IV access
treatments.why: Immediate glucose correction. Onset in seconds. Recheck glucose in 15 min — may need repeat

treatments.med: Glucagon 1mg IM if no IV access
treatments.why: Stimulates hepatic glycogenolysis. Slower onset (10-15 min). Won't work if glycogen depleted (alcoholics, liver failure)

diagnostics.lookingFor: Rule out hemorrhage. Ischemic stroke may be CT-negative early
Large vessel occlusion for potential thrombectomy
Rule out hypoglycemia first — it mimics stroke

differentials.distinguishing: Focal neurological deficits: face droop, arm drift, speech changes (BE-FAST). Sudden onset. LKN time is critical. Posterior strokes may present as isolated AMS/vertigo.

treatments.why: Fibrinolytic dissolves clot. 0.9 mg/kg, 10% bolus, rest over 60 min. Strict contraindication checklist

treatments.intervention: Thrombectomy for large vessel occlusion (up to 24 hrs)
treatments.why: Mechanical clot retrieval. Dramatically better outcomes for LVO strokes. Requires transfer to comprehensive stroke center if not available

diagnostics.lookingFor: ≥2 mmol/L indicates tissue hypoperfusion
Identify source and organism
Pneumonia as source

differentials.distinguishing: AMS may be the ONLY sign in elderly sepsis — they may not mount fever or tachycardia. Look for infection source: UTI (most common in elderly), pneumonia, skin/soft tissue. SIRS criteria.

treatments.intervention: SEP-1 bundle: lactate, cultures, antibiotics within 1 hr, 30 mL/kg crystalloid if hypotensive
treatments.why: Every hour of delay in antibiotics increases mortality. Start the clock at recognition
Cover gram-positive, gram-negative, and anaerobes until cultures return

diagnostics.lookingFor: Electrolyte derangements, renal failure, hepatic encephalopathy (ammonia)
UTI — most common cause of delirium in elderly
New medications, anticholinergics, benzodiazepines, opioids — all cause delirium in elderly

differentials.distinguishing: Acute onset, fluctuating course, inattention (can't spell WORLD backward, can't do months backward). DIFFERENT from dementia (chronic, stable). Always has an underlying CAUSE — find it.

treatments.intervention: Treat the underlying cause
treatments.why: Delirium is a symptom, not a diagnosis. Fix the UTI, correct the sodium, stop the offending med, treat the pain

treatments.intervention: Non-pharmacologic measures first: reorient, glasses/hearing aids, mobilize, sleep hygiene
treatments.why: Antipsychotics (haloperidol) only for severe agitation threatening safety. Benzos worsen delirium (except in alcohol withdrawal)

presentations.arrival: 45M, T 39.2°C, rigors, tachycardia, looks sick, immunocompetent

diagnostics.lookingFor: ≥2 = tissue hypoperfusion, ≥4 = severe sepsis/septic shock
Pan-culture to identify source. Don't delay antibiotics for cultures
Leukocytosis or leukopenia, bandemia, organ dysfunction (creatinine, bilirubin)

differentials.distinguishing: Fever + SIRS criteria + suspected infection. Tachycardia, tachypnea, hypotension, AMS. Source may not be obvious initially — pan-culture and treat empirically.

treatments.intervention: Hour-1 bundle: lactate, cultures, broad-spectrum antibiotics, 30 mL/kg fluid if hypotensive or lactate ≥4
treatments.why: Time-to-antibiotics is the most modifiable factor in sepsis survival

treatments.med: Norepinephrine drip if MAP <65 after fluid resuscitation
treatments.why: First-line vasopressor for septic shock. Alpha-1 vasoconstriction restores SVR

diagnostics.lookingFor: Elevated WBC (neutrophils = bacterial, lymphocytes = viral), elevated protein, decreased glucose (bacterial), gram stain + culture

diagnostics.test: CT head before LP if indicated
diagnostics.lookingFor: Only if: AMS, focal neuro deficits, papilledema, immunocompromised, seizure. Do NOT delay antibiotics for CT/LP
Positive in ~50% of bacterial meningitis — get before antibiotics but don't delay

differentials.distinguishing: Fever + headache + neck stiffness (nuchal rigidity). Photophobia, AMS, petechial rash (meningococcal). Kernig and Brudzinski signs (low sensitivity but specific). Can deteriorate rapidly.

treatments.why: Empiric bacterial coverage. Dexamethasone BEFORE or WITH first antibiotic dose — reduces inflammation, improves outcomes (especially Strep pneumo). Do NOT delay antibiotics for LP or CT

treatments.intervention: Droplet precautions until meningococcal ruled out
treatments.why: N. meningitidis is transmitted via respiratory droplets. Close contacts need prophylaxis (rifampin or ciprofloxacin)

diagnostics.lookingFor: Pyuria, bacteriuria, positive nitrites, positive leukocyte esterase
Leukocytosis, assess renal function (may have AKI from obstruction or sepsis)

differentials.distinguishing: Fever + flank pain + costovertebral angle tenderness (CVA tenderness). Dysuria/frequency may or may not be present. More systemic than simple UTI — rigors, nausea, vomiting.

treatments.med: Ceftriaxone 1g IV (if admitting) or fluoroquinolone PO (if discharging)
treatments.why: Cover gram-negative uropathogens (E. coli most common). Check local antibiogram for resistance patterns

treatments.intervention: IV fluids + antipyretics
treatments.why: Dehydration from fever/vomiting. Acetaminophen or ibuprofen for fever and comfort

presentations.arrival: 62M, witnessed collapse at church, brief LOC (~30 seconds), now alert and oriented, no seizure activity

diagnostics.lookingFor: Bradycardia, heart block, prolonged QTc, Brugada pattern, WPW (delta wave), VT
Intermittent arrhythmias that caused the syncope — may be paroxysmal
ACS as cause of arrhythmia

differentials.distinguishing: No prodrome (sudden), exertional syncope, palpitations before event, syncope while sitting/supine, family history of sudden cardiac death. ECG abnormalities. HIGH-RISK — admit for monitoring.

treatments.intervention: Continuous cardiac monitoring + cardiology consult
treatments.why: Cardiac syncope has significant mortality risk. Need to identify and treat the arrhythmia

treatments.intervention: If bradycardic: atropine → pacing per ACLS bradycardia algorithm
treatments.why: Symptomatic bradycardia causing syncope needs immediate rate support

diagnostics.lookingFor: Normal — rules out cardiac cause
Drop ≥20 systolic or ≥10 diastolic on standing

differentials.distinguishing: Clear prodrome: lightheadedness, warmth, nausea, tunnel vision, diaphoresis BEFORE the event. Triggered by prolonged standing, emotional stress, pain, heat. Brief LOC with rapid full recovery. BENIGN — but must rule out cardiac causes first.

treatments.intervention: Reassurance + education on triggers and counterpressure maneuvers
treatments.why: Vasovagal syncope is benign. Teach: sit or lie down at prodrome onset, leg crossing with tensing, adequate hydration/salt intake

treatments.intervention: Safe for discharge if ECG normal, no cardiac risk factors, classic prodrome
treatments.why: Low-risk syncope doesn't need admission. Follow up with PCP. Consider cardiology if recurrent.

diagnostics.lookingFor: Filling defect — syncope from PE indicates large/central clot with transient drop in cardiac output
Sinus tach, S1Q3T3, right heart strain, new RBBB

differentials.distinguishing: Syncope + dyspnea + tachycardia. Risk factors for VTE. Syncope from PE = significant hemodynamic compromise from a large clot. High mortality if missed.

treatments.med: Heparin drip immediately if high suspicion
treatments.why: Don't wait for CT if clinical picture strongly suggests PE with hemodynamic compromise

conditions.title: Sepsis
conditions.overview: Life-threatening organ dysfunction caused by a dysregulated host response to infection. Septic shock = sepsis + vasopressors needed to maintain MAP ≥65 despite adequate fluid resuscitation + lactate >2. Early recognition and treatment is the single biggest factor in survival.
conditions.pathophysiology:
### The Cascade
- Infection triggers **massive inflammatory response** (cytokines, complement, coagulation)
- Vasodilation → **distributive shock** (warm shock initially, cold shock late)
- Capillary leak → third-spacing → **relative hypovolemia**
- Microvascular thrombosis → **tissue hypoxia** → organ dysfunction
- Myocardial depression → decreased cardiac output

### Through the DO2/VO2 Lens
- DO2 drops: CO decreases (myocardial depression) + maldistribution of blood flow
- VO2 increases: metabolic demand surges from infection/inflammation
- Supply-demand mismatch → **lactate production** → lactic acidosis
conditions.keyFeatures:
### SIRS Criteria (MultiCare screening)
- Temp >38.0°C or <36.0°C
- HR >90
- RR >20 or PaCO2 <32
- WBC >12,000 or <4,000 or >10% bands

### Sepsis Red Flags
- **Altered mental status** (confusion, agitation)
- **Hypotension** (SBP <90 or MAP <65)
- **Lactate >2 mmol/L**
- **Mottled skin**, delayed cap refill
- **Decreased urine output** (<0.5 mL/kg/hr)
conditions.management:
### Hour-1 Bundle (SEP-1)
- **Lactate** — draw immediately, repeat if >2
- **Blood cultures** x2 before antibiotics (don't delay abx for cultures)
- **Broad-spectrum antibiotics** within 1 hour
- **30 mL/kg crystalloid** for hypotension or lactate ≥4 (start immediately)
- **Vasopressors** (norepinephrine first-line) if MAP <65 after fluids

### Reassess
- Repeat lactate if initial >2 — trending down = good
- Reassess volume status — passive leg raise, pulse pressure variation
- Target: MAP ≥65, UOP ≥0.5 mL/kg/hr, lactate normalizing
conditions.nursingPriorities:
- **Time zero = recognition** — start the clock when SIRS + suspected infection identified
- 2 large-bore IVs — run fluids wide open
- Blood cultures BEFORE antibiotics but do NOT delay antibiotics to draw cultures
- Strict I&O — Foley catheter for UOP monitoring
- Serial lactate trending
- Continuous hemodynamic monitoring — watch for fluid responsiveness vs fluid overload
- Central line for vasopressors (peripheral norepi is acceptable temporarily per current evidence)
- **Communicate with provider** if MAP stays <65 despite 30 mL/kg — needs pressors
pearls: **Lactate is a perfusion marker, not an infection marker** — it reflects tissue hypoxia from any cause. A rising lactate in treated sepsis means the patient is losing ground.
**Don't withhold fluids because 'the lungs sound wet'** — sepsis is distributive shock. The fluid is third-spacing, not staying intravascular. Give the 30 mL/kg and reassess.
**Altered mental status may be the only sign** — elderly and immunocompromised patients may not mount fever or tachycardia. Confusion + infection = sepsis until proven otherwise.

conditions.title: STEMI
conditions.overview: Complete thrombotic occlusion of a coronary artery causing transmural (full-thickness) myocardial necrosis. Time-critical emergency — every minute of occlusion kills more myocardium. "Time is muscle."
conditions.pathophysiology: Atherosclerotic plaque rupture or erosion exposes subendothelial collagen → platelet adhesion (via vWF) → platelet activation and aggregation → fibrin mesh formation → complete coronary occlusion → downstream myocardium loses oxygen supply → ischemia progresses to necrosis from endocardium outward (wavefront phenomenon). Necrosis is irreversible within 20-40 minutes without reperfusion. Infarcted tissue releases troponin, CK-MB, and inflammatory mediators.
conditions.keyFeatures: Crushing substernal chest pressure radiating to left arm/jaw/back. Diaphoresis, nausea, dyspnea. Women and diabetics may present atypically (fatigue, dyspnea, back pain, indigestion). ECG shows ST elevation in contiguous leads with reciprocal ST depression. Troponin rises within 3-6 hours.
conditions.management: Activate cath lab immediately (Code STEMI). Door-to-balloon goal <90 minutes. ASA 325mg chewed + P2Y12 inhibitor + heparin drip. Nitroglycerin SL if not hypotensive and not inferior MI. Morphine only if pain uncontrolled (controversial — may worsen outcomes). Post-PCI: dual antiplatelet therapy, statin, beta-blocker, ACE inhibitor. Risk stratify with TIMI score.
conditions.nursingPriorities: 12-lead ECG within 10 minutes of arrival. Two large-bore IVs. Continuous cardiac monitoring. Serial troponins. Right-sided ECG if inferior STEMI (leads II, III, aVF) — determines if nitro/diuretics are safe. Monitor for reperfusion arrhythmias post-PCI. Strict I&O. Emotional support — this is terrifying for patients.
pearls: Inferior STEMI (II, III, aVF) — get a right-sided ECG. Right ventricular involvement means the patient is preload-dependent: NO nitro, NO diuretics, give fluids.
"Time is muscle" — every 30-minute delay in reperfusion increases mortality. Treat ECG acquisition like a vital sign.
Wellens syndrome (deep symmetric T-wave inversions in V2-V3) = critical LAD stenosis about to occlude. Patient may feel fine between episodes. This is a pre-STEMI warning.
Post-discharge 5-drug bundle: ASA + P2Y12 + statin + beta-blocker + ACE inhibitor. Know why each one is there.

conditions.title: Pulmonary Embolism
conditions.overview: Thrombus (usually from DVT) lodges in the pulmonary vasculature, obstructing blood flow through the lungs. Ranges from incidental subsegmental PE to massive PE with hemodynamic collapse (obstructive shock). Time-critical — massive PE kills fast.
conditions.pathophysiology:
### The Obstruction
- Clot lodges in pulmonary artery → **dead space ventilation** (ventilated but not perfused lung)
- Increased pulmonary vascular resistance → **RV strain** → RV dilation → RV failure
- RV dilation pushes septum into LV → decreased LV filling → decreased CO → shock
- V/Q mismatch → **hypoxemia**

### Risk Factors (Virchow's Triad)
- **Stasis**: immobility, surgery, long travel, paralysis
- **Endothelial injury**: trauma, surgery, central lines
- **Hypercoagulability**: cancer, OCP, pregnancy, genetic (Factor V Leiden, etc.)
conditions.keyFeatures:
### Classic Presentation
- **Acute dyspnea** (most common symptom)
- **Pleuritic chest pain** (worse with breathing)
- **Tachycardia** — often out of proportion to other findings
- **Hypoxemia** — but SpO2 can be normal in smaller PE
- **Clear lungs** — this is key: tachycardic + dyspneic + clear lungs = think PE

### Massive PE Signs
- Hypotension, syncope, cardiac arrest (PEA)
- JVD, RV heave
- ECG: S1Q3T3 (classic but uncommon), right heart strain, sinus tach
conditions.management:
### Risk Stratification
- **Massive PE** (hypotension/shock): systemic tPA + heparin, consider catheter-directed therapy
- **Submassive PE** (RV strain, normal BP): heparin + close monitoring, consider tPA if deteriorating
- **Low-risk PE**: heparin, transition to DOAC, may be appropriate for outpatient treatment

### Anticoagulation
- **Heparin drip** (weight-based) — start immediately if high clinical suspicion, don't wait for CT
- Transition to DOAC (rivaroxaban, apixaban) for ongoing treatment
- Duration: 3-6 months minimum, longer if unprovoked or recurrent
conditions.nursingPriorities:
- **Oxygen** — high-flow if hypoxemic
- **Continuous monitoring** — watch for RV failure, hypotension
- **Heparin drip** — weight-based dosing, monitor aPTT q6h
- **Prepare for rapid deterioration** — massive PE can arrest without warning
- **If tPA given**: no invasive procedures, watch for bleeding, neuro checks
- **Elevate head of bed**, avoid Valsalva maneuvers
- **Emotional support** — patients with PE are terrified, often feel like they're dying
pearls: **Tachycardia + dyspnea + clear lungs = PE until proven otherwise.** Most other causes of dyspnea have abnormal lung findings.
**Don't wait for CT to start heparin** — if clinical suspicion is high (Wells score), anticoagulate immediately. The CT confirms, the heparin treats.
**PEA arrest in a young person = think massive PE.** Bedside echo showing RV dilation is your clue. tPA can be given during arrest for suspected massive PE.

conditions.title: Acute Ischemic Stroke
conditions.overview: Sudden loss of brain function due to interrupted blood supply to a region of the brain. Either thrombotic (clot forms in situ) or embolic (clot travels from elsewhere, usually the heart in afib). Time-critical — "time is brain." Every minute of occlusion = ~1.9 million neurons lost.
conditions.pathophysiology:
### Ischemic Cascade
- Arterial occlusion → tissue distal to clot loses oxygen
- **Core infarct**: irreversibly damaged tissue (minutes)
- **Penumbra**: ischemic but salvageable tissue surrounding the core — THIS is what we're trying to save
- Without reperfusion, penumbra converts to core over hours

### Why Timing Matters
- tPA window: **within 4.5 hours** of last known normal (LKN)
- Thrombectomy window: **up to 24 hours** for large vessel occlusion with salvageable tissue on imaging
conditions.keyFeatures:
### BE-FAST Screening
- **B**alance — sudden loss of balance/coordination
- **E**yes — sudden vision loss or double vision
- **F**ace — facial droop (ask to smile)
- **A**rm — arm drift (hold both arms out, eyes closed)
- **S**peech — slurred or garbled speech
- **T**ime — note the LKN time

### Important Distinctions
- **LKN (last known normal)** — NOT when symptoms were found. If patient woke up with symptoms, LKN = when they were last seen normal (bedtime)
- Posterior circulation strokes (brainstem/cerebellum): vertigo, ataxia, diplopia — easy to miss
conditions.management:
### Code Stroke Timeline (MultiCare)
- **5 min**: MD at bedside
- **10 min**: CT head started
- **20 min**: CT interpreted
- **35 min**: tPA decision
- **45 min**: tPA administered if indicated

### Thrombolytics
- **Alteplase (tPA)**: 0.9 mg/kg (max 90 mg), 10% as bolus, rest over 60 min
- Within 4.5 hours of LKN
- **Contraindications**: active bleeding, recent surgery, INR >1.7, platelets <100K, BP >185/110

### Thrombectomy
- Large vessel occlusion (ICA, M1, basilar)
- Up to 24 hours with favorable imaging
conditions.nursingPriorities:
- **LKN time is CRITICAL** — ask family, witnesses, check cameras, phone records
- **NIHSS** — perform and document baseline and serial assessments
- **BP management**: keep <185/110 pre-tPA, <180/105 post-tPA x 24 hrs
- **1:1 nursing for 6 hours post-tPA** — neuro checks q15 min x 2 hr, q30 min x 6 hr
- **Watch for hemorrhagic conversion** — sudden headache, worsening neuro exam, vomiting = STOP tPA, stat CT
- **NPO until swallow screen** — aspiration risk is high
- **Blood glucose** — keep 140-180, hypoglycemia worsens outcomes
pearls: **The LKN time drives EVERYTHING** — not when symptoms were noticed, not when EMS was called. If the patient woke up with symptoms, LKN = last time seen normal before sleep.
**Don't drop the BP too fast** — the hypertension is compensatory, maintaining perfusion to the penumbra. Only treat to get below thrombolytic thresholds.
**Posterior circulation strokes are the ones we miss** — dizziness, nausea, ataxia, diplopia don't scream 'stroke' the way face/arm/speech do. Always consider it.

conditions.title: DKA
conditions.overview: Metabolic emergency from absolute or relative insulin deficiency. Hyperglycemia + ketoacidosis + dehydration. Most common in Type 1 DM but occurs in Type 2 with illness/stress. Mortality <1% with proper treatment, but mistreatment (especially K+ mismanagement) kills.
conditions.pathophysiology:
### The Cascade
- **No insulin** = cells can't use glucose = glucose rises in blood
- Body switches to **fat metabolism** = free fatty acids = hepatic ketogenesis = **ketone bodies**
- Ketones are acids = **anion gap metabolic acidosis**
- Hyperglycemia = **osmotic diuresis** = massive fluid loss (3-6L deficit typical)
- Fluid loss = electrolyte depletion (K+, Na+, Phos, Mg2+)

### The Potassium Trap
- Total body K+ is DEPLETED from osmotic diuresis
- But serum K+ may appear **normal or high** because acidosis shifts K+ out of cells
- When you give insulin and correct acidosis, K+ shifts BACK into cells = **life-threatening hypokalemia**
- **DO NOT give insulin if K+ <3.3** = replace K+ first
conditions.keyFeatures:
### Classic Triad
- **Hyperglycemia** (usually >250, often >500)
- **Ketosis** (positive ketones, elevated beta-hydroxybutyrate)
- **Acidosis** (pH <7.3, bicarb <18, anion gap >12)

### Presentation
- Polyuria, polydipsia, nausea/vomiting, abdominal pain
- **Kussmaul respirations** = deep, rapid breathing (compensatory)
- **Fruity breath** (acetone)
- Dehydration, tachycardia, altered mental status
- **Find the trigger**: infection, missed insulin, new-onset DM, MI, steroids
conditions.management:
### MultiCare DKA Protocol
- **Fluids first**: NS 1-2L bolus, then 250-500 mL/hr. Switch to D5 1/2 NS when glucose <200
- **Potassium**: Check BEFORE insulin. K+ <3.3 = replace first, hold insulin. K+ 3.3-5.3 = add 20-40 mEq to each liter. K+ >5.3 = hold K+, recheck q2h
- **Insulin drip**: 0.1-0.14 units/kg/hr. Goal: glucose drop 50-75/hr. Add dextrose when glucose <200. Do NOT stop insulin until anion gap closes
- **Bicarb**: only if pH <6.9
- **Monitor**: BMP q2h, glucose q1h, strict I&O
conditions.nursingPriorities:
- **Check K+ BEFORE starting insulin** = life-saving step
- **Two IVs** = one for fluids, one for insulin drip
- **Hourly glucose checks** = titrate insulin to drop 50-75/hr
- **BMP every 2 hours** = watching K+, bicarb, anion gap
- **Continuous cardiac monitoring** = K+ shifts cause arrhythmias
- **Keep insulin drip until gap closes** = overlap SubQ insulin 2hr before stopping drip
- **Find the trigger** = why did they go into DKA?
pearls: **Check potassium before insulin, every single time.** Giving insulin to a hypokalemic patient can kill them.
**Don't stop the insulin drip when glucose normalizes** = the goal is to close the anion gap. Add dextrose and keep the drip running.
**Abdominal pain in DKA is often the DKA itself**, not a surgical abdomen. It resolves with treatment.

conditions.title: Anaphylaxis
conditions.overview: Severe, life-threatening systemic allergic reaction. IgE-mediated mast cell degranulation causes vasodilation, bronchoconstriction, mucosal edema. Can kill in minutes. EPINEPHRINE IS THE TREATMENT.
conditions.pathophysiology:
### Type I Hypersensitivity
- Prior sensitization = IgE antibodies on mast cells
- Re-exposure = antigen cross-links IgE = **mast cell degranulation**
- **Histamine, leukotrienes, prostaglandins** released:
  - Vasodilation = distributive shock
  - Bronchoconstriction = wheezing, respiratory distress
  - Mucosal edema = airway swelling
  - Capillary leak = urticaria, third-spacing
- Biphasic reactions in 5-20% = can recur 4-12 hours later
conditions.keyFeatures:
### Diagnostic Criteria
- Skin/mucosal involvement (hives, angioedema) + respiratory compromise OR hypotension
- Two or more rapidly after allergen: skin, respiratory, GI, hypotension
- Hypotension after KNOWN allergen

### Common Triggers
- Foods (peanuts, shellfish, tree nuts)
- Medications (antibiotics, NSAIDs, contrast)
- Insect stings (bees, wasps)
- Latex
conditions.management:
### EPINEPHRINE FIRST
- **Epi 0.3-0.5 mg IM** anterolateral thigh. Repeat q5-15 min if no improvement
- **Epi drip** 1-10 mcg/min for refractory cases

### Adjuncts (AFTER epi)
- IV fluids = aggressive for distributive shock
- Albuterol neb = for bronchospasm
- Diphenhydramine 50mg IV = treats urticaria, NOT first-line
- Methylprednisolone 125mg IV = may reduce biphasic reaction
- Prepare for intubation if airway edema progressing
conditions.nursingPriorities:
- **EPI FIRST** = IM anterolateral thigh. Nothing else matters until epi is given
- **Two large-bore IVs** = fluids wide open for shock
- **Continuous monitoring** = watch for airway compromise
- **Observe 4-6 hours** = biphasic reactions
- **Before discharge**: epi auto-injector Rx, allergy referral, action plan
pearls: **Epi is first, epi is second, epi is third.** Benadryl is for hives, not anaphylaxis.
**IM in the thigh, not subQ in the arm.** Anterolateral thigh has fastest absorption.
**Observe 4-6 hours** = biphasic reactions in 5-20% of patients.

conditions.title: Atrial Fibrillation
conditions.overview: Most common sustained arrhythmia. Disorganized atrial electrical activity causes irregular, often rapid ventricular response. Major stroke risk from atrial thrombus formation.
conditions.pathophysiology:
### Electrical Chaos
- Multiple re-entrant wavelets in atria = chaotic depolarization (350-600/min)
- AV node filters irregularly = **irregularly irregular** ventricular rate
- Loss of organized atrial contraction = blood **stasis** = thrombus = stroke

### Hemodynamic Impact
- Loss of atrial kick (15-25% of CO)
- Rapid rate = decreased diastolic filling = decreased CO
- Chronic uncontrolled = tachycardia-mediated cardiomyopathy
conditions.keyFeatures:
### ECG
- **No P waves** = fibrillatory baseline
- **Irregularly irregular** R-R intervals
- Narrow QRS (unless aberrancy or BBB)

### Presentation
- Palpitations, dyspnea, fatigue
- If RVR: chest pain, dizziness, near-syncope
- May be asymptomatic (incidental finding)
- Stroke may be the presenting event
conditions.management:
### Stable or Unstable?
- Unstable (hypotension, AMS, chest pain) = synchronized cardioversion per ACLS

### Rate Control
- **Diltiazem** 0.25 mg/kg IV over 2 min, drip 5-15 mg/hr (first-line)
- **Metoprolol** 5mg IV q5min x3 (alternative, preferred in HFrEF)
- Target HR <110

### Rhythm Control
- Afib <48 hours = may cardiovert
- >48 hours or unknown = need 3+ weeks anticoagulation OR TEE first

### Anticoagulation
- CHA2DS2-VASc score determines need
- Most afib patients need long-term anticoagulation (DOAC preferred)
conditions.nursingPriorities:
- **Rate control is the ED priority** = get HR <110
- **Diltiazem drip**: monitor BP, hold if SBP <90
- **Do NOT cardiovert unknown-duration afib** without anticoag or TEE = stroke risk
- **Look for the trigger**: thyroid, PE, sepsis, alcohol, missed meds
- **Anticoagulation discussion before discharge**
pearls: **New afib = always ask WHY.** Afib is often a symptom of PE, sepsis, thyroid storm, acute HF, or alcohol.
**Never give diltiazem to afib with WPW** (delta waves on ECG). Forces conduction down accessory pathway = can degenerate to VFib.
**Afib with RVR is rate control, not cardioversion** (unless unstable).

conditions.title: Hyperkalemia
conditions.overview: Serum K+ >5.0. Life-threatening when >6.0 or with ECG changes. The heart is exquisitely sensitive to potassium. Treat the ECG, not just the number.
conditions.pathophysiology:
### Why It's Dangerous
- Elevated K+ = resting membrane potential less negative = cells easier to depolarize initially
- Higher levels = Na+ channels inactivate = conduction slows = cardiac arrest
- ECG progression: **peaked T waves = PR prolongation = widened QRS = sine wave = VFib/asystole**

### Common Causes
- **Renal failure** (most common)
- **Medications**: ACE-I, ARBs, K+-sparing diuretics, NSAIDs, TMP-SMX
- **Cell lysis**: rhabdo, tumor lysis, hemolysis, burns
- **Acidosis**: H+ in, K+ out
conditions.keyFeatures:
### ECG Changes (progressive)
- 5.5-6.0: Peaked T waves
- 6.0-7.0: PR prolongation, flattened P waves
- 7.0-8.0: Widened QRS
- >8.0: Sine wave = VFib = asystole

### Symptoms
- Often **asymptomatic** until cardiac arrest
- Weakness, paresthesias, nausea
conditions.management:
### Three Categories
**1. STABILIZE** (minutes)
- **Calcium gluconate 10mL of 10% IV over 2-3 min** = does NOT lower K+, stabilizes heart

**2. SHIFT** (15-30 min, temporary)
- **Insulin 10 units IV + D50 25g** = most effective. ALWAYS give dextrose
- **Albuterol 10-20mg neb** = adjunct
- **Bicarb** = only if acidotic

**3. REMOVE** (hours, definitive)
- **Furosemide** = if kidneys work
- **Kayexalate** = slow, controversial
- **Dialysis** = definitive for renal failure
conditions.nursingPriorities:
- **12-lead ECG immediately** = peaked T waves = treat NOW
- **Calcium gluconate at bedside** if K+ >6.0
- **Give insulin AND dextrose together** = check glucose q30 min
- **Continuous cardiac monitoring**
- **Recheck K+ in 1-2 hours** = shift treatments are temporary
- **Avoid K+-containing fluids (LR)** until resolved
pearls: **Calcium gluconate is a membrane stabilizer, not a K+ treatment.** It buys time while insulin/glucose shifts K+ into cells.
**Always give dextrose with insulin.** Insulin without dextrose = hypoglycemia.
**Peaked T waves = calcium gluconate immediately.** Don't wait for the lab.

anatomy.title: Cardiovascular System
anatomy.keyStructures:
4-chamber pump: RA → RV → lungs → LA → LV → body. Right side is low-pressure (pulmonary), left side is high-pressure (systemic). LV wall is 3x thicker than RV because it pumps against systemic resistance.

4 valves prevent backflow: Tricuspid (RA→RV), Pulmonic (RV→PA), Mitral (LA→LV), Aortic (LV→aorta). AV valves anchored by chordae tendineae to papillary muscles.

Conduction system: SA node (pacemaker, 60-100 bpm) → AV node (40-60 bpm, 0.1 sec delay) → Bundle of His → R/L bundle branches → Purkinje fibers (20-40 bpm).

Coronary circulation: LCA splits to LAD (anterior wall, septum, apex) + LCx (lateral wall). RCA supplies inferior wall, RV, and usually the PDA. Coronaries fill during diastole.
anatomy.normalPhysiology:
CO = HR x SV (normal 4-8 L/min). Stroke volume determined by preload (stretch), afterload (resistance), and contractility (squeeze strength).

BP = CO x SVR. Mean arterial pressure (MAP) = DBP + 1/3(SBP - DBP). Minimum MAP for organ perfusion: ~60 mmHg.

Frank-Starling mechanism: more stretch → more forceful contraction (up to a point — overstretched fibers lose contractile force, which is what happens in heart failure).

Baroreceptor reflex: carotid/aortic stretch receptors detect pressure changes → rapid HR and vasomotor adjustment. RAAS provides long-term BP regulation through volume and vasoconstriction.
anatomy.clinicalAssessment:
Heart sounds: S1 (AV valves close, start of systole), S2 (semilunar valves close, end of systole), S3 (rapid filling — normal in young, pathologic >40 = HF), S4 (atrial kick against stiff ventricle = HTN, AS). Murmurs = turbulent flow.

Auscultation points: Aortic (R 2nd ICS), Pulmonic (L 2nd ICS), Erb's (L 3rd ICS), Tricuspid (L 4th ICS), Mitral (L 5th ICS MCL). Mnemonic: All Patients Enjoy Taking Medicine.

JVD: >4 cm above sternal angle at 45 degrees = elevated right heart pressure. Peripheral pulses 0-4+. Cap refill <3 sec. Orthostatic vitals: drop >20 systolic or >10 diastolic = orthostatic hypotension.
anatomy.labValues: Troponin: gold standard for myocardial injury (rises 3-6 hr, peaks 12-24 hr). BNP/NT-proBNP: heart failure marker (>400 strongly suggests HF). PT/INR: extrinsic pathway, monitors warfarin. aPTT: intrinsic pathway, monitors heparin. D-dimer: fibrinolysis marker, rules out DVT/PE. Lactate: tissue hypoperfusion, shock indicator.
anatomy.clinicalConnection:
In STEMI: LAD occlusion → anterior wall ischemia → troponin release → ST elevation in V1-V4. Frank-Starling fails as infarcted muscle can't contract → decreased CO → cardiogenic shock if large territory. Coronary arteries fill in diastole, so tachycardia (shorter diastole) worsens ischemia. Beta-blockers slow HR partly to protect coronary filling time.

In sepsis: distributive shock → massive vasodilation drops SVR → BP = CO x SVR crashes. Fluids increase preload (Frank-Starling), norepinephrine increases SVR. Lactate rises because tissues aren't getting adequate DO2 despite seemingly adequate BP.

anatomy.title: Respiratory System
anatomy.keyStructures:
### Upper Airway
- Nose/mouth → pharynx → larynx (vocal cords) → trachea
- **Epiglottis** guards the laryngeal inlet during swallowing

### Lower Airway
- Trachea → R/L mainstem bronchi (right is wider, shorter, more vertical — aspiration goes right)
- Bronchi → bronchioles → terminal bronchioles → **respiratory bronchioles → alveolar ducts → alveoli**
- ~300 million alveoli, surface area ~70 m² (tennis court)

### Gas Exchange Unit
- **Alveolar-capillary membrane**: Type I pneumocytes (gas exchange) + capillary endothelium
- Type II pneumocytes produce **surfactant** (reduces surface tension, prevents alveolar collapse)
- Pulmonary capillary blood flow matches alveolar ventilation (V/Q matching)
anatomy.normalPhysiology:
### Ventilation (moving air)
- **Inspiration**: diaphragm contracts (flattens), intercostals lift ribs → negative intrathoracic pressure → air flows in
- **Expiration**: passive at rest (elastic recoil). Active in distress (abdominal muscles, accessory muscles)
- Tidal volume ~500 mL, respiratory rate 12-20, minute ventilation = TV x RR

### Gas Exchange (moving O2/CO2)
- O2 diffuses from alveolus → capillary (high to low partial pressure)
- CO2 diffuses from capillary → alveolus
- **V/Q matching**: ventilation should match perfusion. V/Q mismatch = hypoxemia

### Oxygen Transport
- 98% bound to hemoglobin (SpO2 measures this)
- 2% dissolved in plasma (PaO2 measures this)
- **Oxyhemoglobin dissociation curve**: right shift (fever, acidosis, high CO2) = releases O2 to tissues more easily
anatomy.clinicalAssessment:
### Inspection
- Respiratory rate, pattern, depth
- **Accessory muscle use** (SCM, intercostal retractions) = significant distress
- **Tripod positioning** = severe air hunger
- Skin color: cyanosis (late, unreliable), pallor

### Auscultation
- **Normal**: vesicular (soft, low-pitched, throughout), bronchovesicular (medium, over major bronchi), bronchial (loud, over trachea)
- **Abnormal**: wheezes (narrowed airways), crackles/rales (fluid/atelectasis), rhonchi (secretions), stridor (upper airway obstruction — EMERGENCY), diminished/absent (consolidation, effusion, PTX)

### Key Values
- SpO2 94-99% on room air (88-92% target in COPD)
- ABG: pH 7.35-7.45, PaCO2 35-45, PaO2 80-100, HCO3 22-26
anatomy.labValues:
- **ABG/VBG**: pH, PaCO2, PaO2, HCO3, lactate
- **SpO2**: continuous pulse oximetry (unreliable with poor perfusion, nail polish, CO exposure)
- **ETCO2**: end-tidal CO2 — confirms ETT placement, monitors ventilation (normal 35-45)
- **CXR**: consolidation, effusion, PTX, cardiomegaly
- **CT-PA**: pulmonary embolism
- **D-dimer**: rules out PE (sensitive, not specific)
- **Procalcitonin**: bacterial infection marker (helps differentiate bacterial vs viral pneumonia)
anatomy.clinicalConnection: In PE: clot blocks pulmonary blood flow → V/Q mismatch (ventilation without perfusion = dead space) → hypoxemia. RV afterload increases → RV dilation → decreased LV filling → obstructive shock. Clear lungs on auscultation despite severe dyspnea is the clinical clue.

anatomy.title: Nervous System
anatomy.keyStructures:
### Central Nervous System
- **Cerebrum**: frontal (personality, motor, speech-Broca's), parietal (sensory, spatial), temporal (hearing, memory, speech-Wernicke's), occipital (vision)
- **Cerebellum**: coordination, balance, fine motor
- **Brainstem**: midbrain, pons, medulla — vital functions (breathing, HR, BP, consciousness)
- **Spinal cord**: C1-S5, ascending (sensory) and descending (motor) tracts

### Peripheral Nervous System
- **12 cranial nerves** — CN III (pupil constriction), CN VII (facial movement), CN X (vagus — HR, GI)
- **Spinal nerves**: 31 pairs

### Cerebral Blood Supply
- **Anterior circulation**: ICA → MCA (lateral cortex, most strokes) + ACA (medial cortex)
- **Posterior circulation**: vertebral → basilar → PCA (occipital, brainstem, cerebellum)
- **Circle of Willis**: anastomotic ring connecting anterior and posterior (redundancy)

### Protection
- **Meninges**: dura (tough outer), arachnoid (web-like), pia (adhered to brain)
- **CSF**: cushions brain, produced by choroid plexus, reabsorbed by arachnoid granulations
- **Blood-brain barrier**: tight junctions in cerebral capillaries — limits drug/pathogen entry
anatomy.normalPhysiology:
### Neuron Function
- **Action potential**: resting → depolarization (Na+ in) → repolarization (K+ out)
- **Synaptic transmission**: AP reaches terminal → Ca2+ entry → neurotransmitter release → binds postsynaptic receptor
- Key neurotransmitters: **acetylcholine** (NMJ, parasympathetic), **norepinephrine** (sympathetic), **dopamine** (reward, motor), **GABA** (inhibitory), **glutamate** (excitatory), **serotonin** (mood)

### Intracranial Pressure
- **Monro-Kellie doctrine**: skull is a fixed box containing brain (80%), blood (10%), CSF (10%)
- If one increases, another must decrease or ICP rises
- Normal ICP: 5-15 mmHg. CPP = MAP - ICP (goal CPP >60)
- Cushing's triad (late signs of herniation): hypertension, bradycardia, irregular respirations
anatomy.clinicalAssessment:
### Rapid Neuro Assessment
- **GCS**: Eye (4) + Verbal (5) + Motor (6) = 3-15. ≤8 = cannot protect airway
- **Pupils**: size, shape, reactivity. Fixed/dilated = CN III compression = herniation
- **BE-FAST**: Balance, Eyes, Face, Arm, Speech, Time
- **NIHSS**: 11-item stroke severity scale (0-42)

### Motor/Sensory
- Strength: 0/5 (no movement) to 5/5 (full against resistance)
- **Pronator drift**: arms out, palms up, eyes closed — subtle weakness causes pronation and drift

### Reflexes
- **Babinski**: upgoing toe = upper motor neuron lesion (normal in infants)
- **DTRs**: 0-4+ scale, 2+ = normal
anatomy.labValues:
- **CT head (non-contrast)**: first-line for stroke, hemorrhage, mass. Ischemic stroke may be negative initially
- **CT angiography**: identifies large vessel occlusion for thrombectomy
- **Blood glucose**: ALWAYS check — hypoglycemia mimics stroke perfectly
- **PT/INR**: needed before tPA (must be ≤1.7)
- **CSF analysis**: if meningitis suspected (cell count, glucose, protein, gram stain, culture)
- **EEG**: seizure activity, status epilepticus
- **Troponin**: elevated in SAH and severe TBI (neurogenic stunned myocardium)
anatomy.clinicalConnection: In ischemic stroke: MCA occlusion → contralateral face/arm weakness + speech deficit (if dominant hemisphere). Time is brain — 1.9 million neurons die per minute of occlusion. The penumbra (ischemic but salvageable tissue) is what tPA and thrombectomy aim to rescue. BP management is critical: too-aggressive lowering worsens penumbral perfusion.

anatomy.title: Endocrine System
anatomy.keyStructures:
### Key Glands
- **Hypothalamus**: master regulator, links nervous and endocrine systems
- **Pituitary**: anterior (TSH, ACTH, GH, FSH, LH, prolactin) and posterior (ADH, oxytocin)
- **Thyroid**: T3/T4 (metabolism), calcitonin (calcium)
- **Parathyroid**: PTH (raises calcium)
- **Adrenal cortex**: cortisol (stress), aldosterone (Na+/K+), androgens
- **Adrenal medulla**: epinephrine, norepinephrine (sympathetic)
- **Pancreas**: insulin (beta cells, lowers glucose), glucagon (alpha cells, raises glucose)

### Feedback Axis
- Hypothalamus → releasing hormone → pituitary → stimulating hormone → target gland → hormone → negative feedback to hypothalamus/pituitary
anatomy.normalPhysiology:
### Glucose Regulation
- **Insulin** (beta cells): glucose uptake into cells, glycogen synthesis, fat storage. The ONLY hormone that lowers glucose.
- **Glucagon** (alpha cells): glycogenolysis, gluconeogenesis. Raises glucose.
- **Counter-regulatory hormones** (cortisol, epinephrine, GH): all raise glucose during stress

### Thyroid
- T4 (thyroxine) → converted to T3 (active form) in tissues
- Controls basal metabolic rate, HR, temperature, energy
- TSH feedback: low T3/T4 → high TSH (hypothyroid). High T3/T4 → low TSH (hyperthyroid)

### Adrenal
- **Cortisol**: stress response, anti-inflammatory, glucose mobilization
- **Aldosterone**: Na+ retention, K+ excretion in kidneys (RAAS system)
- Adrenal insufficiency = can't mount stress response = cardiovascular collapse
anatomy.clinicalAssessment:
### Blood Glucose
- Always check in AMS, seizure, weakness, diaphoresis
- Hypoglycemia (<70): tremor, diaphoresis, confusion, seizure
- Hyperglycemia (>250 + ketones + acidosis = DKA)

### Thyroid
- Hyperthyroid: tachycardia, tremor, weight loss, heat intolerance, exophthalmos (Graves)
- Hypothyroid: bradycardia, fatigue, weight gain, cold intolerance, myxedema
- Thyroid storm: extreme hyperthyroidism + fever + tachycardia + AMS = emergency

### Adrenal
- Adrenal crisis: hypotension refractory to fluids/vasopressors + hyponatremia + hyperkalemia. Give hydrocortisone 100mg IV.
anatomy.labValues:
- **Glucose**: fasting 70-100. Random >200 + symptoms = diabetes
- **HbA1c**: 3-month glucose average. <5.7% normal, 5.7-6.4% prediabetes, ≥6.5% diabetes
- **TSH**: 0.5-5.0 mIU/L. High = hypothyroid, Low = hyperthyroid (confusing but remember: feedback)
- **Cortisol**: AM 6-23 mcg/dL. Random cortisol <3 in critical illness = adrenal insufficiency
- **BMP**: glucose, Na+, K+ (aldosterone effects)
- **Anion gap**: elevated in DKA (ketoacids)
- **Beta-hydroxybutyrate**: direct ketone measurement (better than urine ketones)
anatomy.clinicalConnection: In DKA: no insulin → glucose can't enter cells → hyperglycemia → osmotic diuresis → dehydration. Fat is metabolized instead → ketones → anion gap metabolic acidosis. The potassium trap: total body K+ is depleted but serum K+ may look normal because acidosis shifts K+ extracellularly. Giving insulin drives K+ back into cells → potentially fatal hypokalemia.

anatomy.title: Renal System
anatomy.keyStructures:
### Kidney Anatomy
- 2 kidneys, retroperitoneal, ~1 million nephrons each
- **Nephron**: functional unit = glomerulus + tubule system
- **Glomerulus**: filters blood (180 L/day → reabsorbs 99% → 1-2 L urine)
- **Tubules**: PCT (reabsorbs 65% of filtrate), Loop of Henle (concentrates urine), DCT, Collecting duct

### Key Structures
- **Afferent arteriole** → glomerulus → **efferent arteriole** (this arrangement creates filtration pressure)
- **Juxtaglomerular apparatus**: renin-secreting cells that sense perfusion pressure → trigger RAAS
- **Ureters → bladder → urethra**: drainage system
anatomy.normalPhysiology:
### Kidney Functions
- **Filter waste**: creatinine, BUN, toxins, drug metabolites
- **Regulate volume**: Na+ and H2O reabsorption/excretion
- **Regulate electrolytes**: K+, Ca2+, Phos, Mg2+
- **Acid-base balance**: HCO3- reabsorption, H+ excretion
- **Hormone production**: erythropoietin (RBC production), activated vitamin D (calcium absorption), renin (BP regulation)

### GFR
- Glomerular Filtration Rate = how well the kidneys filter
- Normal: >90 mL/min
- Creatinine is the surrogate marker — rising creatinine = falling GFR

### Diuretic Sites of Action
- **Loop diuretics** (furosemide): Loop of Henle, Na-K-2Cl cotransporter. Most potent.
- **Thiazides** (HCTZ): DCT, Na-Cl cotransporter. Moderate.
- **K+-sparing** (spironolactone): Collecting duct, aldosterone antagonist. Weak diuretic but saves K+.
anatomy.clinicalAssessment:
### Urine Output
- Normal: 0.5-1.0 mL/kg/hr
- Oliguria: <0.5 mL/kg/hr — concerning for AKI
- Anuria: <100 mL/24hr — obstruction or severe AKI

### Fluid Assessment
- Overloaded: edema, JVD, crackles, weight gain
- Depleted: tachycardia, hypotension, dry mucous membranes, decreased skin turgor

### Key Labs
- **BUN/Creatinine ratio**: >20:1 suggests prerenal (dehydration, HF), <20:1 suggests intrinsic renal
- Electrolytes: hyperkalemia is the killer in renal failure
anatomy.labValues:
- **Creatinine**: 0.7-1.3 mg/dL. Rising creatinine = declining kidney function
- **BUN**: 7-20 mg/dL. Rises with dehydration, GI bleed, high protein, renal failure
- **GFR**: >90 normal, <60 = CKD stage 3, <15 = ESRD (needs dialysis)
- **Potassium**: 3.5-5.0. Renal failure causes hyperkalemia (can't excrete K+)
- **Urinalysis**: specific gravity (concentration), protein (glomerular damage), blood, WBCs (infection), casts
- **Urine electrolytes**: FENa <1% = prerenal, >2% = intrinsic renal
anatomy.clinicalConnection: In hyperkalemia from renal failure: kidneys can't excrete K+ → serum K+ rises → cardiac membrane destabilization → arrhythmias. Loop diuretics help if the patient still makes urine. If oliguric/anuric, dialysis is the definitive treatment. Many ED medications are renally cleared — dose adjust everything.

medications.title: Antiplatelet Agents
medications.moa:
Inhibit platelet activation and aggregation through different mechanisms:
- COX-1 inhibition (aspirin): blocks thromboxane A2 production, which normally promotes platelet aggregation. Irreversible — lasts the life of the platelet (8-10 days).
- P2Y12 receptor antagonists (clopidogrel, ticagrelor, prasugrel): block ADP-mediated platelet activation. Clopidogrel is irreversible (prodrug, needs hepatic activation). Ticagrelor is reversible (faster offset).
- GP IIb/IIIa inhibitors (eptifibatide, abciximab): block the final common pathway of platelet aggregation. IV only, used in cath lab.
fdaUses: Acute Coronary Syndrome (STEMI, NSTEMI, unstable angina)
Secondary prevention of MI and stroke
nonFdaUses: Primary prevention in very high-risk patients (controversial, USPSTF updated 2022)

edDrugs.name: Aspirin
edDrugs.dose: 325mg chewed (ACS loading), 81mg daily (maintenance)
edDrugs.why: Cornerstone of ALL ACS management. Chewing bypasses first-pass metabolism for faster onset (~15 min vs 60 min swallowed). Contraindicated in true aspirin allergy — use clopidogrel alone.

edDrugs.name: Clopidogrel (Plavix)
edDrugs.dose: 600mg loading dose, then 75mg daily
edDrugs.why: P2Y12 inhibitor for dual antiplatelet therapy. Prodrug — requires CYP2C19 activation, so ~2-hour onset and some patients are poor metabolizers (consider genetic testing). Used when ticagrelor is contraindicated.

edDrugs.name: Ticagrelor (Brilinta)
edDrugs.dose: 180mg loading dose, then 90mg BID
edDrugs.why: Preferred P2Y12 inhibitor in ACS — faster onset, more potent, and reversible (unlike clopidogrel). PLATO trial showed mortality benefit over clopidogrel. Side effects: dyspnea (benign, self-limiting), bradycardia.

edDrugs.name: Prasugrel (Effient)
edDrugs.dose: 60mg loading dose, then 10mg daily
edDrugs.why: Most potent P2Y12 inhibitor — fastest, most consistent platelet inhibition. But highest bleeding risk. Contraindicated in prior stroke/TIA, age >75, weight <60kg. Used selectively in cath lab.

medications.adverseEffects: Bleeding is the major risk across all agents — GI bleeding, intracranial hemorrhage, surgical bleeding. Aspirin: GI ulceration, tinnitus at high doses. Clopidogrel: TTP (rare). Ticagrelor: dyspnea (dose-related, usually tolerable), ventricular pauses. Prasugrel: highest bleeding risk of the P2Y12 agents.
medications.nursingConsiderations: Hold for active bleeding. Check platelet count (thrombocytopenia increases risk). Teach patients: bleeding precautions, soft toothbrush, electric razor, avoid NSAIDs, report blood in stool/urine/dark tarry stools. Do NOT crush ticagrelor (can give via NG if needed). Dual antiplatelet therapy duration: usually 12 months post-ACS/stent, then step down to aspirin alone. Patients must understand they CANNOT stop these medications without talking to cardiology — stent thrombosis risk.

medications.title: Anticoagulants
medications.moa:
Prevent clot formation and propagation through different mechanisms:
- **Unfractionated heparin (UFH)**: potentiates antithrombin III → inhibits thrombin (IIa) and Factor Xa. IV drip for acute anticoagulation.
- **Low-molecular-weight heparin (enoxaparin/Lovenox)**: preferentially inhibits Factor Xa. SubQ dosing, more predictable pharmacokinetics.
- **Warfarin**: inhibits vitamin K-dependent clotting factors (II, VII, IX, X). Oral, takes 3-5 days for full effect.
- **DOACs** (rivaroxaban, apixaban, dabigatran): direct inhibitors of Factor Xa or thrombin. Oral, predictable dosing, fewer monitoring requirements.

edDrugs.name: Heparin (UFH)
edDrugs.dose: VTE: 80 units/kg bolus → 18 units/kg/hr. ACS: 60 units/kg bolus (max 4000) → 12 units/kg/hr
edDrugs.why: Gold standard for acute anticoagulation in ED — fast onset (minutes), short half-life (60-90 min), titratable, reversible with protamine. Weight-based dosing is critical.

edDrugs.name: Enoxaparin (Lovenox)
edDrugs.dose: VTE treatment: 1 mg/kg SubQ q12h. DVT prophylaxis: 40 mg SubQ daily
edDrugs.why: More predictable than UFH, no aPTT monitoring needed. Bridge to oral therapy. Renally cleared — dose adjust for CrCl <30.

edDrugs.name: Warfarin (Coumadin)
edDrugs.dose: 2-10 mg PO daily, titrate to INR goal 2-3
edDrugs.why: Only oral anticoagulant for mechanical heart valves. Takes 3-5 days for full effect — need heparin bridge. Reversed with vitamin K + FFP/PCC for acute bleeding.

edDrugs.name: Rivaroxaban (Xarelto)
edDrugs.dose: DVT/PE: 15 mg BID x 21 days, then 20 mg daily. Afib: 20 mg daily with dinner
edDrugs.why: Direct Xa inhibitor. No monitoring needed. Take with food (increases absorption). Andexanet alfa for life-threatening bleeding reversal.

medications.adverseEffects: **Bleeding** is the primary risk for all anticoagulants. UFH: HIT (heparin-induced thrombocytopenia) — check platelets, if dropping STOP heparin and switch to argatroban. Warfarin: narrow therapeutic index, food/drug interactions (vitamin K-rich foods, CYP interactions). DOACs: GI bleeding (especially dabigatran, rivaroxaban).
medications.nursingConsiderations:
- **Heparin drip**: weight-based protocol, aPTT q6h (goal 60-80 sec or 1.5-2.5x normal), adjust per protocol. Check platelets for HIT.
- **Warfarin**: INR monitoring (goal 2-3 for most indications, 2.5-3.5 for mechanical valves). Teach consistent vitamin K intake.
- **DOACs**: assess renal function (dose adjust or avoid in severe renal impairment). No routine monitoring needed.
- **ALL anticoagulants**: fall risk assessment, bleeding precautions, hold for procedures, know the reversal agents (protamine for heparin, vitamin K + PCC for warfarin, andexanet alfa or PCC for Xa inhibitors, idarucizumab for dabigatran).

medications.title: Antibiotics Overview
medications.moa:
Kill or inhibit bacteria through different mechanisms:
- **Cell wall synthesis inhibitors**: beta-lactams (penicillins, cephalosporins, carbapenems) — most commonly used. Bactericidal.
- **Protein synthesis inhibitors**: macrolides (azithromycin), tetracyclines (doxycycline), aminoglycosides (gentamicin) — bacteriostatic (aminoglycosides are bactericidal).
- **DNA/RNA synthesis inhibitors**: fluoroquinolones (levofloxacin, ciprofloxacin) — bactericidal. Black box warnings.
- **Folate synthesis inhibitors**: trimethoprim/sulfamethoxazole (Bactrim) — bacteriostatic.
- **Cell membrane disruptors**: daptomycin, polymyxins — last-resort agents.
nonFdaUses: Inflammatory conditions (azithromycin for CF, COPD exacerbation)

edDrugs.name: Ceftriaxone (Rocephin)
edDrugs.dose: 1-2g IV daily
edDrugs.why: 3rd-gen cephalosporin — workhorse of the ED. Covers most community-acquired infections: pneumonia, UTI/pyelo, meningitis, gonorrhea. Once-daily dosing. Broad gram-negative coverage + some gram-positive.

edDrugs.name: Vancomycin
edDrugs.dose: 15-20 mg/kg IV q8-12h (weight-based, trough-guided)
edDrugs.why: Covers MRSA and other resistant gram-positives. Used for severe skin/soft tissue, endocarditis, meningitis. Monitor trough levels (goal 15-20 for serious infections). Red man syndrome with rapid infusion — run over at least 60 min.

edDrugs.name: Piperacillin-Tazobactam (Zosyn)
edDrugs.why: Extended-spectrum penicillin + beta-lactamase inhibitor. Very broad coverage: gram-positive, gram-negative, anaerobes. Go-to for sepsis, intra-abdominal, healthcare-associated infections.

edDrugs.name: Azithromycin (Zithromax)
edDrugs.dose: 500mg IV/PO day 1, then 250mg PO days 2-5 (Z-pack); or 500mg IV daily
edDrugs.why: Macrolide — covers atypicals (Mycoplasma, Legionella, Chlamydia). Added to ceftriaxone for CAP. Also used for STIs (chlamydia: 1g single dose). QTc prolongation risk.

medications.adverseEffects:
- **Beta-lactams**: allergic reactions (cross-reactivity ~2% pen → ceph), C. diff risk, seizures (high dose or renal failure)
- **Vancomycin**: nephrotoxicity (especially with piperacillin-tazobactam combo), red man syndrome, ototoxicity
- **Fluoroquinolones**: tendon rupture, QTc prolongation, aortic dissection risk, peripheral neuropathy — BLACK BOX warning, use only when no alternative
- **Aminoglycosides**: nephrotoxicity, ototoxicity — monitor levels
- **ALL**: C. difficile risk, resistance development
medications.nursingConsiderations:
- **Culture before you treat** — but don't delay antibiotics for cultures in sepsis
- **Allergy history is critical** — true penicillin allergy (anaphylaxis, hives) vs intolerance (GI upset). Cross-reactivity with cephalosporins is ~2%, not 10% as previously taught
- **Time matters in sepsis** — every hour of delay in antibiotics increases mortality. Draw cultures, hang antibiotics, don't wait for pharmacy
- **Renal dosing** — many antibiotics need dose adjustment for renal impairment (vancomycin, aminoglycosides, carbapenems)
- **Monitor for C. diff** — new diarrhea on antibiotics = send stool sample
- **Complete the course** — undertreating breeds resistance

medications.title: Beta-Blockers
medications.moa:
Block beta-adrenergic receptors, reducing sympathetic stimulation:
- **Beta-1** (heart): decrease HR (chronotropy), decrease contractility (inotropy), decrease AV conduction (dromotropy), decrease renin release
- **Beta-2** (lungs, vessels): bronchodilation, vasodilation. Blocking beta-2 = bronchoconstriction (why beta-blockers are cautious in asthma)

Selective (cardioselective) agents: metoprolol, atenolol, esmolol — preferentially block beta-1. Selectivity is dose-dependent.
Non-selective: propranolol, carvedilol, labetalol — block both beta-1 and beta-2. Labetalol also blocks alpha-1.
fdaUses: Heart failure with reduced EF (carvedilol, metoprolol succinate, bisoprolol)
nonFdaUses: Thyroid storm (propranolol — blocks peripheral T4→T3 conversion)
Aortic dissection (esmolol — rate and BP control)

edDrugs.name: Metoprolol (Lopressor/Toprol)
edDrugs.dose: IV: 5mg q5min x3. PO tartrate: 25-100mg BID. PO succinate: 25-200mg daily
edDrugs.why: Tartrate = short-acting for acute rate control. Succinate = extended-release for chronic HF (ONLY succinate has mortality benefit in HFrEF). Most commonly used beta-blocker in the ED.

edDrugs.name: Esmolol (Brevibloc)
edDrugs.dose: 500 mcg/kg bolus over 1 min, then 50-200 mcg/kg/min drip
edDrugs.why: Ultra-short acting (half-life 9 min). Perfect when you need rate/BP control but are worried about side effects — if patient crashes, stop drip and it clears in minutes. First-line for aortic dissection.

edDrugs.name: Labetalol
edDrugs.dose: 10-20mg IV push q10min (max 300mg), or drip 0.5-2 mg/min
edDrugs.why: Alpha + beta blockade = both HR and BP control. Good for hypertensive emergencies, preeclampsia. Don't use in acute decompensated HF (negative inotropy without alpha blockade compensation).

edDrugs.name: Propranolol
edDrugs.dose: 1mg IV q2min (max 3mg for SVT). PO: 10-40mg TID-QID
edDrugs.why: Non-selective. Used in thyroid storm (blocks peripheral T4→T3 AND controls HR). Lipophilic = crosses BBB = helps with anxiety, tremor.

medications.adverseEffects: Bradycardia, hypotension, bronchospasm (beta-2 blockade — caution in asthma/COPD), fatigue, depression, masking of hypoglycemia symptoms (blocks tachycardia response), sexual dysfunction, rebound tachycardia if stopped abruptly.
medications.nursingConsiderations:
- **Hold if HR <60 or SBP <90** — assess before each dose
- **Do NOT give in acute decompensated HF** — negative inotropy worsens pump failure. Beta-blockers are for STABLE chronic HFrEF only.
- **Asthma/COPD caution** — beta-1 selective agents (metoprolol) are safer but selectivity is dose-dependent
- **Do NOT stop abruptly** — rebound tachycardia/hypertension, can precipitate MI. Taper over 1-2 weeks
- **Monitor glucose in diabetics** — beta-blockers mask hypoglycemia symptoms (tremor, tachycardia)
- **Esmolol drip**: dedicated line, titrate to HR goal, watch BP closely

medications.title: Insulin
medications.moa:
Binds insulin receptors on cell membranes → activates GLUT4 transporters → glucose uptake into cells. Also:
- Promotes glycogen synthesis (stores glucose)
- Promotes fat storage (lipogenesis)
- Promotes protein synthesis
- **Shifts K+ into cells** (clinically important — used to treat hyperkalemia)
- Inhibits hepatic gluconeogenesis and glycogenolysis

Types differ by **onset, peak, and duration** — from rapid-acting (minutes) to long-acting (24+ hours).
nonFdaUses: Calcium channel blocker overdose (high-dose insulin/euglycemic therapy — insulin has inotropic effects)

edDrugs.name: Regular insulin (Humulin R)
edDrugs.dose: DKA drip: 0.1-0.14 units/kg/hr. Hyperkalemia: 10 units IV + D50 25g. SubQ sliding scale: varies
edDrugs.why: The ONLY insulin given IV. Used for DKA drips and hyperkalemia. Clear solution — do NOT confuse with other insulin types.

edDrugs.name: Insulin lispro (Humalog) / aspart (NovoLog)
edDrugs.dose: SubQ: per sliding scale or carb counting. Onset 15 min, peak 1-2 hr, duration 3-5 hr
edDrugs.why: Rapid-acting — used for meal coverage and correction doses. Given SubQ only. Used in insulin pumps.

edDrugs.name: Insulin glargine (Lantus) / detemir (Levemir)
edDrugs.dose: SubQ: once daily (glargine) or BID (detemir). Dose varies by patient
edDrugs.why: Long-acting basal insulin. Provides 24-hour baseline coverage. No peak (glargine) — reduces hypoglycemia risk. Never give IV. Never adjust in the ED without endocrine guidance.

medications.adverseEffects: **Hypoglycemia** is the major and potentially fatal adverse effect. Weight gain. Injection site lipodystrophy. Hypokalemia (when used for hyperkalemia — always co-administer dextrose).
medications.nursingConsiderations:
- **DKA drip**: check glucose hourly, BMP q2h, check K+ BEFORE starting. Goal glucose drop 50-75/hr — if dropping faster, reduce rate. Do NOT stop until anion gap closes.
- **Hyperkalemia**: 10 units regular + D50 25g together. Recheck glucose q30 min (hypoglycemia risk). Recheck K+ q1-2h (effect is temporary).
- **Sliding scale**: check glucose before meals and at bedtime. Give correction dose per protocol. Notify provider if glucose >300 or <70.
- **Never give long-acting insulin IV** — it's designed for SubQ absorption
- **High-alert medication** — always have a second nurse verify dose and type. Insulin errors are a leading cause of inpatient medication harm.

medications.title: Bronchodilators
medications.moa:
Open airways through two mechanisms:
- **Beta-2 agonists** (SABA/LABA): stimulate beta-2 receptors on bronchial smooth muscle → relaxation → bronchodilation. Also increase mucociliary clearance.
- **Anticholinergics** (SAMA/LAMA): block muscarinic M3 receptors → reduce acetylcholine-mediated bronchoconstriction and mucus secretion.

SABA/SAMA = short-acting rescue. LABA/LAMA = long-acting maintenance.
nonFdaUses: Hyperkalemia (high-dose albuterol neb as K+ shifter)

edDrugs.name: Albuterol (ProAir, Ventolin)
edDrugs.dose: Neb: 2.5 mg q4-6h PRN; acute: q20min x3 or continuous. MDI: 2 puffs q4-6h
edDrugs.why: SABA — the rescue inhaler. Fastest-acting bronchodilator (onset 5 min). First-line for all acute bronchospasm. Side effects: tachycardia, tremor, hypokalemia.

edDrugs.name: Ipratropium (Atrovent)
edDrugs.dose: Neb: 0.5 mg q6-8h; MDI: 2 puffs QID
edDrugs.why: SAMA — anticholinergic. Slower onset (15-30 min). Add to albuterol for first 3 nebs in severe asthma/COPD exacerbation. DuoNeb = albuterol + ipratropium combo.

edDrugs.name: Racemic epinephrine
edDrugs.dose: Neb: 0.5 mL of 2.25% solution in 3 mL NS
edDrugs.why: Alpha-mediated mucosal vasoconstriction reduces airway edema. Used for croup (stridor) and post-extubation stridor. Monitor for rebound swelling 2-4 hours after administration.

medications.adverseEffects: Beta-2 agonists: tachycardia, tremor, hypokalemia (drives K+ into cells), hyperglycemia, anxiety. Anticholinergics: dry mouth, urinary retention, narrow-angle glaucoma risk (nebulized — use mouthpiece or mask that avoids eyes).
medications.nursingConsiderations:
- **Albuterol continuous neb** for severe asthma: monitor HR (will be tachycardic), K+ (can drop significantly)
- **Ipratropium + albuterol** for first 3 treatments in severe exacerbation, then albuterol alone
- **Assess response**: breath sounds, work of breathing, SpO2, peak flow (if able). Improving = good. Silent chest = bad (not enough air movement to wheeze)
- **COPD: target SpO2 88-92%** — don't blast them with 100% O2 (blunts hypoxic drive, worsens V/Q mismatch)
- **Spacer for MDIs** — dramatically improves drug delivery. If patient can't coordinate MDI, use neb

medications.title: Diuretics
medications.moa:
Increase urine output by blocking sodium reabsorption at different points in the nephron:
- **Loop diuretics** (furosemide, bumetanide): block Na-K-2Cl cotransporter in thick ascending limb of Loop of Henle. Most potent. Waste K+, Ca2+, Mg2+.
- **Thiazides** (HCTZ, chlorthalidone): block Na-Cl cotransporter in distal convoluted tubule. Moderate potency. Waste K+, retain Ca2+.
- **K+-sparing** (spironolactone, eplerenone): block aldosterone in collecting duct. Weak diuretic. Save K+. Spironolactone has mortality benefit in HFrEF.
- **Osmotic** (mannitol): osmotic gradient draws water into tubule. Used for cerebral edema.
fdaUses: Heart failure (loop diuretics for volume overload)
nonFdaUses: Ascites (spironolactone + furosemide in 100:40 ratio for cirrhosis)

edDrugs.name: Furosemide (Lasix)
edDrugs.dose: IV: 20-80mg; acute HF: home PO dose given IV, or 2.5x if no response. Max single dose ~200mg
edDrugs.why: Workhorse loop diuretic. IV onset 5 min, PO 30-60 min. 'Lasix = lasts six hours.' First-line for acute decompensated HF and pulmonary edema.

edDrugs.name: Bumetanide (Bumex)
edDrugs.dose: IV/PO: 0.5-2mg. 1mg bumetanide = 40mg furosemide
edDrugs.why: 40x more potent mg-for-mg than furosemide. Better oral bioavailability. Used when furosemide response is inadequate (gut edema impairs absorption).

edDrugs.name: Spironolactone (Aldactone)
edDrugs.dose: HF: 25-50mg PO daily. Ascites: 100mg PO daily (with furosemide 40mg)
edDrugs.why: Aldosterone antagonist — K+-sparing. Mortality benefit in HFrEF (RALES trial). Also first-line for cirrhotic ascites. Monitor K+ closely — hyperkalemia risk.

medications.adverseEffects: Loop: hypokalemia, hypomagnesemia, hypocalcemia, ototoxicity (rapid IV push), dehydration, metabolic alkalosis. Thiazides: hypokalemia, hyponatremia, hyperuricemia (gout), hypercalcemia. K+-sparing: hyperkalemia, gynecomastia (spironolactone).
medications.nursingConsiderations:
- **Monitor K+ and Mg2+** with all loop/thiazide diuretics. Replace aggressively.
- **Strict I&O** — document urine output after each dose. Poor response = may need higher dose or continuous drip
- **Daily weights** — most accurate measure of fluid balance (1 kg = 1 liter)
- **IV push furosemide slowly** (over 1-2 min) — rapid push causes ototoxicity
- **Assess for dehydration** — overdiuresis causes prerenal AKI, hypotension
- **Spironolactone + ACE inhibitor** = hyperkalemia risk. Check K+ within 1 week of starting

principles.title: Electricity and Excitability
principles.theCore:
Every nerve signal, muscle contraction, and heartbeat is an **electrical event**.

- Cells maintain a **voltage difference** across their membranes (resting membrane potential) by actively pumping ions
- When stimulated, **ion channels open** → ions rush across the membrane → voltage changes = **action potential**
- In the heart, this electrical wave coordinates the mechanical pump
- The whole system depends on **ion concentrations** — which is why electrolyte imbalances are so dangerous
principles.howItWorks:
### Resting Membrane Potential
- **Na+/K+ ATPase pump** maintains resting potential: pumps 3 Na+ out, 2 K+ in
- Creates net negative charge inside: **-70mV** (neurons), **-90mV** (cardiac cells)
- K+ is the dominant ion — it sets the resting voltage

### Cardiac Action Potential Phases
- **Phase 0** — Rapid depolarization: Na+ channels open, Na+ rushes in *(this is the QRS on ECG)*
- **Phase 1** — Brief repolarization: Na+ channels close, some K+ exits
- **Phase 2** — Plateau: Ca2+ enters through L-type channels *(triggers contraction; this is the ST segment)*
- **Phase 3** — Repolarization: K+ channels open, K+ exits, Ca2+ channels close *(T wave)*
- **Phase 4** — Return to resting potential

### SA Node Automaticity
- SA node cells have **no true resting potential** — Phase 4 slowly depolarizes on its own (funny current, If)
- This is **automaticity** — why the heart beats without external input
- Intrinsic rate: SA node 60-100 bpm → AV node 40-60 → Purkinje 20-40
principles.clinicalConnection:
### Electrolyte Effects on Electrical Activity
- **K+ (potassium)** — dominant ion for resting membrane potential
  - Hyperkalemia: peaked T waves → widened QRS → sine wave → arrest
  - Hypokalemia: U waves, QT prolongation, torsades risk
- **Ca2+ (calcium)** — essential for plateau phase and contraction
  - Hypocalcemia: prolonged QT
  - Hypercalcemia: shortened QT
- **Mg2+ (magnesium)** — stabilizes ion channels
  - Hypomagnesemia makes arrhythmias harder to treat
  - **Always check and replace Mg2+ alongside K+**

### Antiarrhythmic Drug Classes
- **Class I** — Na+ channel blockers (slow depolarization)
- **Class II** — Beta-blockers (slow SA/AV nodes)
- **Class III** — K+ channel blockers (prolong repolarization)
- **Class IV** — Ca2+ channel blockers (slow AV conduction)
principles.atTheBedside:
### Peaked T Waves → Think Hyperkalemia
- **Calcium gluconate** is first-line — stabilizes the cardiac membrane (raises threshold potential) without changing K+ level
- Buys you time while **insulin/glucose** and other treatments shift K+ back into cells
- It's a **membrane stabilizer**, not a K+ treatment

### Torsades de Pointes → Give Magnesium
- **Mg2+ stabilizes the cell membrane** and shortens repolarization
- Breaks the cycle of triggered activity driving the arrhythmia
- 2g IV magnesium sulfate over 10-20 min

### Complete Heart Block → Pacing
- SA node signal can't reach ventricles
- Ventricle relies on **escape rhythm** (20-40 bpm) — too slow
- External pacemaker provides the electrical stimulus the conduction system can't deliver
- **Transcutaneous pacing** as bridge to **transvenous pacing**

principles.title: Hemodynamics (Flow and Pressure)
principles.theCore:
All of emergency medicine hemodynamics comes down to one equation: **BP = CO x SVR**.

- **Blood Pressure** = the pressure perfusing your organs
- **Cardiac Output** = how much blood the heart pumps per minute (CO = HR x SV)
- **SVR** = how tight the blood vessels are

Every intervention in shock — fluids, vasopressors, inotropes, blood — is manipulating one of these variables.
principles.howItWorks:
### Cardiac Output = HR x SV
- **Heart Rate**: too slow = low CO (bradycardia), too fast = decreased filling time = low CO (tachycardia)
- **Stroke Volume** determined by:
  - **Preload**: how much blood fills the ventricle (stretch). Frank-Starling: more stretch = more force (up to a point)
  - **Afterload**: resistance the heart pumps against (SVR for LV, PVR for RV)
  - **Contractility**: intrinsic squeeze strength (inotropy)

### MAP = DBP + 1/3(SBP - DBP)
- Minimum MAP for organ perfusion: **~60-65 mmHg**
- Below this: kidneys stop making urine, brain gets confused, gut becomes ischemic

### Starling Forces (capillary level)
- **Hydrostatic pressure** pushes fluid OUT of capillary
- **Oncotic pressure** (albumin) pulls fluid IN
- Imbalance = edema (HF, nephrotic syndrome, liver failure)
principles.clinicalConnection:
### Shock = Inadequate Tissue Perfusion
- **Hypovolemic**: low preload (bleeding, dehydration). Tx: volume
- **Cardiogenic**: pump failure (MI, HF). Tx: inotropes, reduce afterload
- **Distributive**: vasodilation (sepsis, anaphylaxis). Tx: vasopressors + volume
- **Obstructive**: mechanical obstruction (PE, tamponade, tension PTX). Tx: fix the obstruction

Every vasopressor targets a specific receptor to manipulate these variables:
- **Norepinephrine**: alpha-1 (vasoconstriction = increases SVR) + some beta-1 (increases CO). First-line for septic shock.
- **Epinephrine**: beta-1 (CO) + beta-2 (bronchodilation) + alpha-1 (SVR). Anaphylaxis, cardiac arrest.
- **Phenylephrine**: pure alpha-1 (SVR only). When you need vasoconstriction without cardiac stimulation.
principles.atTheBedside:
### Reading the Numbers
- **Low BP + high HR + low CVP** = hypovolemic shock. Give fluids.
- **Low BP + high HR + high CVP + JVD** = cardiogenic or obstructive. Don't flood them with fluids — they need inotropes or the obstruction fixed.
- **Low BP + high HR + warm extremities** = distributive (sepsis). Start vasopressors after initial fluid bolus.

### Fluid Responsiveness
- Not every hypotensive patient needs more fluid
- **Passive leg raise** (45-degree elevation): if BP improves by >10%, they're fluid-responsive
- After 30 mL/kg in sepsis, reassess before giving more — fluid overload kills too

principles.title: Oxygen Delivery and Consumption (DO2/VO2)
principles.theCore:
The entire point of the cardiovascular and respiratory systems is to deliver oxygen to tissues. **DO2 = CO x CaO2**.

- **DO2** = oxygen delivery (how much O2 reaches the tissues)
- **CO** = cardiac output (the pump)
- **CaO2** = arterial oxygen content = **(Hgb x SaO2 x 1.34) + (PaO2 x 0.003)**

The Hgb-bound portion dominates. This means: Hgb level, SaO2, and cardiac output are the three levers for oxygen delivery.
principles.howItWorks:
### The DO2 Equation
- **DO2 = CO x [(Hgb x SaO2 x 1.34) + (PaO2 x 0.003)]**
- Normal DO2: ~1000 mL O2/min
- Normal VO2 (consumption): ~250 mL O2/min
- **O2 extraction ratio**: VO2/DO2 = normally ~25% (we have 4x reserve)

### Three Levers to Fix DO2
- **Increase CO**: fluids (preload), vasopressors, inotropes
- **Increase Hgb**: transfuse PRBCs (each unit raises Hgb ~1 g/dL)
